Post_Text: Two schools took the same storm, same week, same county. One reopened in three weeks. The other fought its carrier for a year. Same damage. Same coverage. Here is what split them: [LINK]
Two schools took the same storm. One reopened in three weeks. The other fought its carrier for a year. (Full newsletter, see linkedin-newsletter.md)

Your carrier does not pay because the building is wrecked. It pays because you can prove what you had and what it was worth. A property claim is a documentation contest, and you win it before the loss: [LINK]

The adjuster asks what was in the wing the fire took. "A lot of expensive stuff" is not a number they can cut a check against. A dated photo and a value is. This is the inventory almost every school skips: [LINK]

A property claim is a documentation contest you win before the loss, or you do not win it at all. Here is the readiness most schools discover they are missing the morning after. (Checklist + matrix, see company-page-checklist.md)

Post_Text: Your insurance policy is in a filing cabinet inside the building you are insuring. If the building burns, so does the proof of what you bought. Where your dec page lives matters more than you think: [LINK]

Most wind and named-storm deductibles are not a flat dollar figure. They are a percentage of the building's value, which is a much bigger number than people expect. If you cannot explain yours right now, read this before the next storm: [LINK]

Your property policy obligates you to limit the damage after a loss. Let the standing water sit a week and the carrier can cut what it pays for the mold. The rule most school leaders were never told: mitigate fast, photograph first. [LINK]

Picture the worst Tuesday. A fire, a flood, a roof opened by the wind, tonight. Could you hand someone a current inventory, your policy from outside the building, and a one-page plan of who does what? Or would you be starting from scratch in the wreckage? [LINK]
